vocabulary.meaning: in a kind, generous, and caring way toward others
vocabulary.example: She acted benevolently, donating much of her salary to the local shelter.

vocabulary.meaning: something that encourages or motivates a person to do something
vocabulary.example: Tax breaks gave the company a strong incentive to invest in new technology.

vocabulary.meaning: officially gives a right, honor, or quality to someone
vocabulary.example: Ownership of the land confers the right to build a house on it.

vocabulary.meaning: to give up or let go of something, often unwillingly
vocabulary.example: The owner was forced to relinquish part of his land for the new highway.

vocabulary.meaning: takes property from its owner, usually for public use or by force
vocabulary.example: Marx argued that the capitalist expropriates the labor of the worker.

idioms.meaning: to assume something is true or always available without questioning or appreciating it.
examples: People often take clean water for granted until a drought arrives.
In the text: the idea of private property is “often taken for granted,” yet the author says nothing about it is inevitable.

idioms.meaning: to make something unpleasant, harsh, or disappointing easier to accept.
examples: A small bonus helped soften the blow of the pay cut.
In the text: the 14th Amendment softens the blow of losing property by requiring the owner to be paid the market price.

reading.text: We learn about ownership and personal property from our earliest childhood tussles over toys. This concept is often taken for granted, yet there is nothing inevitable about the idea. Private property is fundamental to capitalism. Karl Marx noted that the wealth generated by capitalism presents societies with “an immense collection of commodities” that are privately owned and may be traded for profit. Businesses are also privately owned and operated for profit in a free market. Without the idea of private property, there is no potential for personal gain—there is no reason even to enter the market. There is, in effect, no market.

“Property” encompasses a wide range of things, from material goods to intellectual property (such as patents or written text). It has entered realms that even free market economists would not defend, such as slavery—where people were viewed as commodities.

Historically, material property has been organized three different ways. First, everything can be held in common and used by everyone as they wish, on the basis of mutual trust and custom. This was the case in tribal economies, and it is still practiced by the Huaorani people of the Amazon. Second, property can be held and used collectively; this is the essence of the communist system. Third, property can be held in private, with each person free to do with it as they choose. This is the concept at the heart of capitalism.

Modern economists tend to justify private property on pragmatic grounds, arguing that the market simply can’t operate without some division of resources. Earlier thinkers made more of a moral case for private property. The Greek philosopher Aristotle argued that “property should be private.” He pointed out that when property is held in common, no one takes responsibility to maintain and improve it. Moreover, people can only become generous if they have something to give away.

In the 17th century all land and housing in Europe was effectively owned by monarchs. The English philosopher John Locke (1632–1704), however, spoke out for individual rights, saying that as God gave us dominion over our own bodies, we also have dominion over the things we make. The German philosopher Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) later argued that private property is a legitimate expression of the self.

Another German philosopher, however, rejected the notion of private property entirely. Karl Marx insisted that the concept of private property is nothing but a device by which the capitalist expropriates the labor of the proletarian, keeps him in slavery, and excludes him. The proletariat is effectively locked out of the select group that controls all wealth and power.

reading.title: How private?
reading.text:
In every modern society some things are shared as collective property, such as streets and parks. Others, such as cars, are private property. Property rights, or legal ownership, normally confer on the owner exclusive rights over a particular resource, but this is not always the case. The owner of a house in a historic district, for instance, might not be allowed to knock it down and replace it with a skyscraper or a factory, or even change the use of the current building.

The governments of every country in the world reserve the right to override private ownership when this is deemed necessary, for reasons varying from the needs of infrastructure to national safety issues. Even in the US, a staunchly capitalist nation, the government may force a property owner to relinquish his or her rights. However, the 14th amendment to the Constitution softens this blow by stating that the owner must be compensated with the market price.
It is clearly better that property should be private, but the use of it common; and the special business of the legislator is to create in men this benevolent disposition.

mcq.stem: According to Aristotle, what is one problem with property that is held in common?
options: No one takes responsibility to maintain or improve it.
It makes people too generous with their belongings.
It forces governments to build more infrastructure.
It increases trade and investment between individuals.
mcq.explain: The text says that when property is held in common, “no one takes responsibility to maintain and improve it.”

mcq.stem: Why does the text say people can only become generous if property is private?
options: Because private owners pay lower taxes.
Because people can only give away things they actually own.
Because the government rewards generous owners.
Because common property is easier to share.
mcq.explain: The passage states that people “can only become generous if they have something to give away”—that is, only if they own it.

mcq.stem: What did Karl Marx believe about private property?
options: It is a legitimate expression of the self.
It should be protected by every government.
It is a device the capitalist uses to exploit the worker.
It is necessary for people to act benevolently.
mcq.explain: Marx insisted private property is “a device by which the capitalist expropriates the labor of the proletarian” and keeps him excluded.

mcq.stem: According to the box “How private?”, what may a government do to a private owner?
options: Ban the owner from ever selling the property.
Override private ownership when deemed necessary, with compensation.
Take the property without giving any payment.
Force the owner to share the property with neighbors.
mcq.explain: Governments may override private ownership when deemed necessary, but the 14th Amendment requires the owner be compensated at the market price.

mcq.stem: Which statement best matches Aristotle’s quoted view at the bottom of the page?
options: All property should be owned collectively.
Property should be private, but its use should be common.
Property should belong only to the government.
Private property should be completely abolished.
mcq.explain: Aristotle’s quote says it is better “that property should be private, but the use of it common.”

discussion.stem: The text describes three ways property has historically been organized (held in common, held collectively, held in private). Which system do you think works best, and why?
discussion.hint: Think about who is responsible for maintaining the property, how people are motivated to work or trade, and whether resources are shared fairly. There is no single “correct” system—state your choice and back it up.
discussion.sample: I think a mostly private system works best, because the text points out that when no one owns something, no one takes responsibility to maintain or improve it, and there is no incentive to invest. However, I would keep some things—like streets, parks, and clean water—held in common, since everyone depends on them. A mix of private ownership with some shared resources balances motivation with fairness.

discussion.stem: Aristotle said property should be private but its use should be common. Do you agree that this is possible in real life? Explain your view.
discussion.hint: Look for real examples where something is privately owned but shared in use—libraries, community gardens, ride-sharing, lending a tool to a neighbor. Then consider what could go wrong (people not taking care of shared things).
discussion.sample: I partly agree. It is possible in some situations: a person can own a car privately but share rides, or a family can own a house and welcome guests to use it. Charity and lending work this way too. But it is hard to make it work on a large scale, because people tend to take less care of things they do not own. So Aristotle’s idea is realistic for small communities built on trust, but it needs good laws and a generous attitude to work widely—which is exactly the “benevolent disposition” he says lawmakers should encourage.

discussion.stem: The 14th Amendment says owners must be compensated when the government takes their property. Do you think this rule is fair to both the owner and the public? Why or why not?
discussion.hint: Consider both sides—the owner who loses their property against their will, and the public who may need the land for roads, schools, or safety. Is paying market price enough to make it fair?
discussion.sample: I think the rule is mostly fair, because it protects both sides. The public benefits when the government can build needed infrastructure like roads or hospitals, while the owner is not simply robbed—they receive the market price for what they lose. However, it is not perfectly fair, since the owner may not want to sell at any price, and “market price” does not pay for emotional value or the trouble of moving. Overall it is a reasonable compromise between individual rights and the common good.
